subject: Chemistry
q: A student performed a reaction between hydrogen (\[\ce{H2}\]) and iodine (\[\ce{I2}\]) to form hydrogen iodide (\[\ce{HI}\]). After a while, \[\ce{HI}\] began to decompose into \[\ce{H2}\] and \[\ce{I2}\]. The reaction continued and after some time, the concentrations of all substances became constant. What does it mean?
options: The reaction did not reach equilibrium.
There is not enough information provided to answer.
skill: Discuss reversible reactions and dynamic equilibrium with examples
feedback: It was a reversible reaction which reached equilibrium. In reversible reactions, reactants can form products and products can also re-form reactants. When a reaction reaches equilibrium, the rate of forward reaction becomes equal to the rate of reverse reaction, which is why the concentration of all substances become constant.

subject: Chemistry
q: A student performed a reaction between sodium hydroxide (\[\ce{NaOH}\]) and hydrochloric acid (\[\ce{HCl}\]) to form sodium chloride (\[\ce{NaCl}\]) and water (\[\ce{H2O}\]). After a while, the reaction stopped and only \[\ce{NaCl}\] and \[\ce{H2O}\] were present in the reaction. What does it mean?
options: There is not enough information provided to answer.
skill: Discuss reversible reactions and dynamic equilibrium with examples
feedback: It was an irreversible reaction. At equilibrium, both reactants and products are formed at equal rates. In this reaction, however, the products (\[\ce{NaOH}\] and \[\ce{HCl}\]) did not convert back to the reactants (\[\ce{NaCl}\]) and (\[\ce{H2O}\]). Therefore, it is not a reversible reaction.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statements is FALSE about a reversible reaction?
options: In a reversible reaction, a mixture of products and reactants is formed.
Reversible reactions proceed in forward and reverse directions.
Reversible reactions can be sensitive to pressure and temperature.
A reversible reaction is a complete reaction.
skill: Discuss reversible reactions and dynamic equilibrium with examples
feedback:
A reaction is referred as a complete reaction if at least one of the available reactants is completely converted into products.
A reversible reaction is not a complete reaction, because the reactants never completely convert into products.

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the main difference between reversible and irreversible reactions?
options: The products react to re-form the reactants in a reversible reaction, but in an irreversible reaction they do not react with each other at all.
Reactants react to form products in an irreversible reaction, but in a reversible reaction they do not.
We cannot obtain the products of a reversible reaction as they are mixed with their reactants.
An irreversible reaction is sensitive to temperature and pressure, but a reversible reaction is not.
skill: Discuss reversible reactions and dynamic equilibrium with examples
feedback:
An irreversible is a unidirectional reaction, in which reactants react to form products. But an irreversible reaction is a two-way reaction, in which reactants react to form products and products react to re-form the reactants.

Both reversible and irreversible reactions may be sensitive to temperature and pressure.

We can always separate different compounds from a mixture through different physical changes.

subject: Chemistry
q:
How can we disturb a chemical equilibrium?

I.   by adding a catalyst
II.  by increasing the concentration of the reactants
III. by increasing the concentration of the products
skill: Discuss reversible reactions and dynamic equilibrium with examples
feedback:
In the state of chemical equilibrium, the actual amounts of products and reactants do not change. We can disturb this equilibrium by increasing or decreasing the concentration of the products or the reactants.

If we increase the concentration of the reactants, the forward reaction will speed up and more products will be produced, and vice versa. Similarly, if we take out an amount of the products from the equilibrium mixture, the forward reaction will speed up to produce more of the products.

The addition of a catalyst does not affect the equilibrium. Catalysts only help in speeding up the forward reactions in the beginning of the reaction and decrease the time taken to reach equilibrium.

q:
The neutralisation of potassium hydroxide (\[\ce{KOH}\]) with sulphuric acid (\[\ce{H2SO4}\]) forms potassium sulphate (\[\ce{K2SO4}\]) and water (\[\ce{H2O}\]) as shown below.

\[\ce{2 KOH + H2SO4 <=> K2SO4 + 2 H2O}\]

Given that the neutralisation is exothermic, what will be the effect of decreasing the temperature of the system at equilibrium?
options: The rate of forward reaction will increase.
The rate of reverse reaction will increase.
There will be no effect on the rate of either forward or reverse reaction.
There is not enough information provided to answer.
topic: The equilibrium constant and its unit
skill: Describe the necessary conditions for equilibrium and the ways through which it can be recognised
feedback: The rate of forward reaction will increase. Since the temperature of the system is decreased, the system will respond in such a way to increase the temperature again in order to restore equilibrium. Since the neutralisation is exothermic which releases heat, the rate of forward reaction will increase to facilitate neutralisation and increase the heat of the system.

q: For the reversible reaction, \[K_{c}=\frac{|C|^{2}}{|A||B|}\], which substance is a product of the reaction\[?\]
topic: Law of mass action and derivation of the expression for the equilibrium constant
skill: State the law of mass action and derive an expression for the equilibrium constant and its units
feedback:
The equilibrium constant K\[_{c}\] = \[ \frac{ \mbox{Product of the concentration of the products in the reaction} ^{ \mbox{number of coefficients}} } { \mbox {Product of the concentration of reactants in the reaction} ^{ \mbox {number of coefficients}}}\].

So in the reaction \[K_{c}=\frac{|C|^{2}}{|A||B|}\], substance C will be the product.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Derive the expression for the equilibrium constant and its unit for the following chemical equation?

N2 + H2 \[^{⇀}_{↽}\] 2NH3
topic: Law of mass action and derivation of the expression for the equilibrium constant
skill: State the law of mass action and derive an expression for the equilibrium constant and its units
feedback:
Note that the given chemical equation is not balanced. The balanced chemical equation is
N2 + 3H2 \[^{⇀}_{↽}\] 2NH3.
So,
Kc = [NH3]2 / [N2][H2]3
Now we can calculate the unit using this expression.
unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)2 / [(mol dm-3)(mol dm-3)3]
unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)2 / (mol dm-3)4
unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)-2

So

unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)2 / (mol dm-3)3
unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)-1

feedback:
Note that the given chemical equation is not balanced. In order to derive the expression and the unit of its equilibrium constant, we first need to balance the given chemical equation. The balanced chemical equation is:
CO + 3H2 \[^{⇀}_{↽}\] CH4 + H2O
So,
Kc = [CH4][H2O] / [CO][H2]3
Now we can calculate the unit using this expression.
unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)2 / [(mol dm-3)(mol dm-3)3]
unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)2 / (mol dm-3)4
unit of Kc = (mol dm-3)-2

subject: Chemistry
q:
Calculate the reaction quotient (\[Q_{c}\]) for the following reaction and the concentrations:

\[\ce{2CO + O2 <=> 2CO2}\]

substance concentration (mol dm-3)
CO2 0.0540
CO 0.352
O2 0.110
skill: Discuss the importance of the equilibrium constant, and its macroscopic characteristics
feedback:
The correct value is \[0.214 \ mol^{-1} \ dm^{3}\]. It is calculated as follows along with its units:

\begin{align} Q_{c} &= \frac{[\ce{CO2}]^2}{[\ce{CO}]^2 \ [\ce{O2}]} \ \ \frac{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^{2}}{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^2 \ [mol \ dm^{-3}]}\\ &= \frac{[0.0540]^2}{[0.352]^2 \ [0.110]} \ \ \frac{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^{2}}{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^3}\\ &= 0.214 \ mol^{-1} \ dm^{3} \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q:
Calculate the reaction quotient (\[Q_{c}\]) for the following reaction and the concentrations:

\[\ce{2N2O5 <=> 4NO2 + O2}\]

substance concentration (mol dm-3)
N2O5 3.651
NO2 4.561
O2 1.789
skill: Discuss the importance of the equilibrium constant, and its macroscopic characteristics
feedback:
The correct value is \[58.1 \ mol^{3} \ dm^{-9}\]. It is calculated as follows along with its units:

\begin{align} Q_{c} &= \frac{[\ce{NO2}]^{4}[\ce{O2}]}{[\ce{N2O5}]^2} \ \ \frac{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^{4}[mol \ dm^{-3}]}{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^2}\\ &= \frac{[4.561]^{4}[1.789]}{[3.651]^2} \ \ \frac{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^{5}}{[mol \ dm^{-3}]^2}\\ &= 58.1 \ mol^{3} \ dm^{-9} \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: The production of ammonia (\[\ce{NH3}\]) from nitrogen (\[\ce{N2}\]) and hydrogen (\[\ce{H2}\]) was carried out at two different temperatures: reaction A at 100°C and reaction B at 450°C. The equilibrium constant (\[K_{c}\]) for both reactions was \[0.0623 \ mol^{-2} \ dm^{6}\] and \[4001.6 \ mol^{-2} \ dm^{6}\], respectively. What conclusions can be made?
options: None of the reactions are at completion.
skill: Discuss the importance of the equilibrium constant, and its macroscopic characteristics
feedback: Reaction B is at completion. Since \[K_{c}\] is much greater than 1, it means that the reaction is almost complete as there are far more products than reactants present. When \[K_{c}\] is much less than 1, it means that the equilibrium favours the formation of reactants as the rate of reverse reaction is higher than the rate of forward reaction. When \[K_{c}\] is equal to one, neither the formation of products nor the formation of products is favoured as the rates of both reactions are equal.

subject: Chemistry
q:
At the equilibrium, the concentrations of substances and the value of \[K_{c}\] were recorded as shown below. What is the concentration of \[\ce{XeF2}\]?

\[\ce{I2 + 3XeF2 <=> 2 IF3 + 3 Xe} \ \ \ K_{c} = 151.4 \ mol \ dm^{-3}\]

substance concentration (mol dm-3)
I2 2.07
IF3 16.5
Xe 24.7
skill: Discuss the importance of the equilibrium constant, and its macroscopic characteristics
feedback:
The concentration of \[\ce{XeF2}\] is 23.6 mol dm-3. It is calculated as follows:

\begin{align} K_{c} \ &= \frac{[\ce{IF3}]^{2} \ [\ce{Xe}]^{3}}{[\ce{I2}] \ [\ce{XeF2}]^{3}}\\ 151.4 &= \frac{[16.5]^2 \ [24.7]^3}{[2.07] \ [\ce{XeF2}]^3} \\ \sqrt[3]{[\ce{XeF2}]^{3}} &= \sqrt[3]{\frac{[16.5]^2 \ [24.7]^3}{[2.07] \ 151.4}} \\ [\ce{XeF2}] &= 23.6 \ mol \ dm^{-3} \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: Bushra forgets to label two beakers that contain two different solutions, A and B. They could be NaOH, HCl, or HNO<sub>3</sub>. She finds out that both A and B conduct electricity, but only B tastes bitter. Solution A turns red litmus paper blue, but B does not change the colour of the blue litmus paper. What can Bushra conclude?
options: A = HCl or HNO<sub>3</sub>; B = NaOH
A = NaOH; B = HCl or HNO<sub>3</sub>
skill: Exemplify Arrhenius acids and bases and discuss its limitations
feedback: Acids turn blue litmus paper red; bases turn red litmus paper blue. Both acids and bases conduct electricity, but bases taste bitter and acids have a sour taste. Since both HCl and HNO3 are acids, it is not possible to distinguish between these two using the tests that Bushra performed. Therefore, solution A could be HCl or HNO3. Solution B is a base, and NaOH is the only base from the options.

skill: Exemplify Arrhenius acids and bases and discuss its limitations
feedback: Acids are corrosive, and they taste sour. In addition, they turn the colour of blue litmus paper red and have no effect on red litmus paper itself. These properties suggest that the new compound is an Arrhenius Acid.

subject: Chemistry
q:
From the reaction shown below, identify the Arrhenius acid(s) and Arrhenius base(s).
\[\ce{H_2CO_3 <=>[H_2O] CO_3^{2-} + 2H^+}\]
skill: Exemplify Arrhenius acids and bases and discuss its limitations
feedback: In this reaction, carbonic acid (H2CO3) dissociates into H+ ions in the presence of water; therefore, it is an Arrhenius acid. Water (H2O) is not an Arrhenius base because it does not dissociate into OH- ions.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which type of compounds can be classified into Arrhenius acids and/or bases?

  1. all ionic compounds
  2. all covalent compounds
  3. ionic compounds containing hydrogen atoms and hydroxyl groups only
skill: Exemplify Arrhenius acids and bases and discuss its limitations
feedback:
Not all ionic compounds can be Arrhenius acids and/or bases. For example, NaCl is an ionic compound containing Na+ ions and Cl- ions, but it does not contain a hydrogen (H+) ion. Covalent compounds do not ionise when dissolved in water (with some exceptions), so they are neither Arrhenius acids nor bases.

Therefore, only ionic compounds that contain ionisable hydrogens and hydroxyl groups can be classified as Arrhenius acids and bases, respectively.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following compounds are neither an Arrhenius acid nor Arrhenius base?

  1. CH3OH (methanol)
  2. KOH (Potassium hydroxide)
  3. H3PO4 (phosphoric acid)
skill: Exemplify Arrhenius acids and bases and discuss its limitations
feedback:
Arrhenius acids are IONIC compounds that dissociate into H+ ions in water. Arrhenius bases are IONIC compounds containing a hydroxyl (OH) group that dissociates into hydroxide ions (OH-) in water.

CH3OH can be confused as an Arrhenius base since it contains a hydroxyl group, but it is NOT an ionic compound. CH3OH is a covalent compound, and it will not dissociate into OH- ions in water. KOH and H3PO4 are ionic compounds, and they are an Arrhenius base and acid, respectively.
KOH is composed of K+ and OH- ions, and it acts as an Arrhenius base.
H3PO4 is composed of three H+ and one PO43- ions, and it acts as an Arrhenius acid.

subject: Chemistry
q: Scientists have discovered a new gas, HX. X belongs to group 7 of the periodic table. Which of the following is CORRECT?
options: X<sup>-</sup> acts as a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry base
X<sup>-</sup> acts as a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acid
skill: State the Bronsted-Lowry theory, and classify substances as acids, bases or proton donors or acceptors, discussing its limitations
feedback:
Notice that X- comes from HX (H+ and X-). Since X is in group 7, it is a halogen like chlorine (Cl), fluorine (F), bromine (Br).
X- will act as a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry base by accepting a proton. It cannot act as an acid because it does not have a proton to donate. Hence, it cannot be considered amphoteric either.
X- + H2O → HX + OH-

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the name of the process and type of base that is industrially manufactured to be used in fertilizers?
skill: State the Bronsted-Lowry theory, and classify substances as acids, bases or proton donors or acceptors, discussing its limitations
feedback: Haber process involves the production of ammonia which is commonly used in manufacturing fertilizers. Ammonia (NH<sub>3</sub>) is NOT an Arrhenius base since it does not have hydroxyl (OH) groups that ionize into OH<sup>-</sup> ions. However, NH<sub>3</sub> accepts a proton, therefore it is a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry base.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following compounds are NOT Arrhenius and Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry bases?
skill: State the Bronsted-Lowry theory, and classify substances as acids, bases or proton donors or acceptors, discussing its limitations
feedback: Arrhenius bases must contain hydroxyl (OH) groups that ionize into hydroxide (OH<sup>-</sup>) ions. The only compound that does not contain an OH group here is PH<sub>3</sub>. H<sub>2</sub>O is an amphoteric compound so it can act as both, an acid and a base. NaOH and Ca(OH)<sub>2</sub> are also Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry bases since they have (OH<sup>-</sup>) ions that can accept protons.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statement(s) is FALSE about Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acid-base theory?

  1. All Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acids become negatively charged after donating protons.
  2. All Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry bases become positively charged after accepting protons.
  3. Acids can also act as bases depending on the chemical reaction with other compounds.
skill: State the Bronsted-Lowry theory, and classify substances as acids, bases or proton donors or acceptors, discussing its limitations
feedback:
It is a common mistake to believe that all Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acids become negatively charged after donating protons and all Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry bases become positively charged after accepting protons.

For example, in the reaction shown below, HS- is the Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry base which accepts a proton from H2O and becomes neutral H2S.
HS- + H2O \[\rightleftharpoons\] H2S + OH-

Similarly, in another reaction shown below, H3O+ is the Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acid which donates the proton to CH3OH and becomes neutral H2O.
H3O+ + CH3OH \[\rightleftharpoons\] H2O + CH3OH2+

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following reactions do NOT contain acids and bases according to Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry theory?

  1. CH3COOH + CH3NH2 \[\rightleftharpoons\] CH3COO- + CH3NH3+
  2. ClO- + 2HClO → ClO3- + 2 HCl
  3. CH3COOH + HS- \[\rightleftharpoons\] CH3COO- + H2S
skill: State the Bronsted-Lowry theory, and classify substances as acids, bases or proton donors or acceptors, discussing its limitations
feedback:
Reaction I and III contain Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acids and bases.
Reaction I CH3COOH donates proton to CH3NH2, acting as a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acid. CH3NH2 accepts the proton and forms CH3NH3+, acting as a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry base.
Reaction III CH3COOH donates a proton to HS-, acting as a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry acid. HS- accepts the proton and becomes H2S, acting as a Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry base.

Reaction II There is no proton transfer which is a requirement for Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry theory.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statements is FALSE?
options: The Arrhenius theory defines acids and bases by their ability to ionise into H<sup>+</sup> and OH<sup>-</sup> ions respectively.
The Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry theory defines acids and bases by their ability to donate and accept protons respectively.
The Lewis theory defines acids and bases by their ability to accept and donate electrons respectively.
The Arrhenius theory is the most flexible theory, whereas the Lewis theory provides the most limited definition of acids and bases.
skill: State Lewis' concepts of acids and bases and classify substances as Lewis' acids or bases
feedback: This question requires you to analyse how three different theories define acids and bases. The Arrhenius theory is the most limited definition because it only includes those compounds that contain ionisable hydrogen atoms and hydroxyl groups, whereas, the Br\[\phi\]nsted-Lowry theory is limited to protons (hydrogen ions) only. The Lewis theory is the most widely accepted one, because electrons are common in all compounds so all of them can be defined as acidic or basic depending on their ability to accept or donate electrons.

subject: Chemistry
q: A new metal X has been discovered which has two valence electrons. Which of the following is true about the reaction between X sulphate and ammonia in an aqueous environment?
options: X sulphate acts as a Lewis acid.
X sulphate acts as a Lewis base.
X sulphate acts as an Arrhenius base.
skill: State Lewis' concepts of acids and bases and classify substances as Lewis' acids or bases
feedback:
Since X has two valence electrons, it will dissociate into X2+ and SO42- ions. Ammonia, NH3, will donate its lone-pair to X2+, acting as a Lewis base.
Therefore, X2+ is a Lewis acid as it accepts electrons.
X2+ + NH3 → [X(NH3)]2+

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the Lewis acid and base in the reaction shown below.

AgCl + 2NH3 → Ag(NH3)2+ + Cl-
skill: State Lewis' concepts of acids and bases and classify substances as Lewis' acids or bases
feedback: Notice again that AgCl is an ionic compound composed of Ag+ and Cl- ions. By looking at the reactants side of the reaction ONLY, it shows that ammonia NH3 donates its lone-pair to Ag+ in AgCl, forming a Ag(NH3)2+ complex ion. Therefore, AgCl is a Lewis acid since it accepts the electrons from ammonia NH3, which is a Lewis base.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the Lewis acid and base in the reaction shown below.

AlCl3 + 6H2O → [Al(H2O)6]3+ + 3Cl-
skill: State Lewis' concepts of acids and bases and classify substances as Lewis' acids or bases
feedback: Notice that AlCl3 is an ionic compound composed of Al3+ and Cl- ions. Oxygen in H2O has lone pairs (acts as a base) which are donated to Al3+ ions in AlCl3 (acting as an acid). Even though Cl- can act as a base, students should look carefully at the reactants side of the reaction. In the reactants, Al3+ ions accept the electrons from O in H2O.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the Lewis acid and base in the reaction shown below.

CH3Cl + NaOH → CH3OH + NaCl
skill: State Lewis' concepts of acids and bases and classify substances as Lewis' acids or bases
feedback: Remember that NaOH is an ionic compound composed of Na+ ions and OH- ions. OH- ions act as a Lewis base since they have excess electrons. They donate electrons to the carbon, C, in CH3Cl, breaking the C—Cl bond and forming a C—OH bond. NaCl is a salt that neither accepts nor donates electrons. It is a product of the reaction, whereas Lewis acids and bases are found in the reactants of this reaction.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Calcium hydroxide (\[\ce{Ca(OH)2}\]) is standardized with 15.0 ml of 1.50 M sulphuric acid (\[\ce{H2SO4}\]) using phenolphthalein indicator. The chemical equation for the reaction and the volume of \[\ce{ Ca(OH)2}\], used (in ml) are shown below.

\[\ce{Ca(OH)2 + H2SO4 -> CaSO4 + 2 H2O}\]

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3 Trial 4
Initial volume (ml) 98.6 87.0 74.0 62.5
Final volume (ml) 87.5 75.7 63.1 51.5


What is the concentration of \[\ce{Ca(OH)2}\]?
skill: Solve problems based on acid-base titrations, and give equations for neutralisation reactions
feedback:
The correct concentration of \[\ce{Ca(OH)2}\] is 2.03 M.

The first step is to calculate the volume of \[\ce{Ca(OH)2}\] used in each trial to neutralise (\[\ce{H2SO4}\]). This is done by subtracting the initial volume from the final volume as shown below:

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3 Trial 4
Initial volume (ml) 98.6 87.0 74.0 62.5
Final volume (ml) 87.5 75.7 63.1 51.5
Used volume (ml) \[98.6 - 87.5 = 11.1\] \[87.0 - 75.7 = 11.3\] \[74.0 - 63.1 = 10.9\] \[62.5 - 51.5 = 11.0\]


The second step is to find the average volume of \[\ce{Ca(OH)2}\] by summing up the individual volumes and dividing them with the number of trials as shown below:

\[\text{Average = }\frac{11.1 + 11.3 + 10.9 + 11.0}{4} = 11.1 \text{ ml}\]

The third step is to use the titration formula to find the concentration of \[\ce{Ca(OH)2}\].

\[\frac{M_{1}V_{1}}{n_{1}}=\frac{M_{2}V_{2}}{n_{2}}\]

Since 1 mole of \[\ce{H2SO4}\] reacts with 1 mole of \[\ce{Ca(OH)2}\], as stated in the equation, the value of \[n_1\] is 1, and the value of \[n_2\] is 1.

\[\frac{(1.50)(15.0)}{1}=\frac{(M_2)(11.1)}{1}\]

\[M_{2}=\frac{1.50\times 15.0}{11.1} = 2.03\text{ M}\]

subject: Chemistry
q:
Sodium hydroxide, \[\ce{NaOH}\], is standardized with 50.0 ml of 0.95 M hydrochloric acid, \[\ce{HCl}\], using phenolphthalein indicator. The chemical equation for the reaction and the volume of \[\ce{NaOH}\], used (in ml) are shown below.

\[\ce{NaOH + HCl -> NaCl + H2O}\]

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3
Initial volume (ml) 67.1 45.0 25.0
Final volume (ml) 47.1 25.2 5.0


What is the concentration of \[\ce{NaOH}\]?
skill: Solve problems based on acid-base titrations, and give equations for neutralisation reactions
feedback:
The correct concentration of \[\ce{NaOH}\] is 2.38 M.

The first step is to calculate the volume of \[\ce{NaOH}\] used in each trial to neutralise HCl. This is done by subtracting the initial volume from the final volume as shown below:

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3
Initial volume (ml) 67.1 45.0 25.0
Final volume (ml) 47.1 25.2 5.0
Used volume (ml) \[67.1 - 47.1 = 20.0\] \[45.0 - 25.2 = 19.8\] \[25.0 - 5.0 = 20.0\]


The second step is to find the average volume of \[\ce{NaOH}\] by summing up the individual volumes and dividing them with the number of trials, as shown below:

\[\text{Average = }\frac{20.0 + 19.8 + 20.0}{3} = 19.9 \text{ ml}\]

The third step is to use the titration formula to find the concentration of \[\ce{NaOH}\].

\[\frac{M_{1}V_{1}}{n_{1}}=\frac{M_{2}V_{2}}{n_{2}}\]

Since 1 mole of \[\ce{HCl}\] reacts with 1 mole of \[\ce{NaOH}\], as stated in the equation, the value of \[n_1\] is 1, and the value of \[n_2\] is 1.

\[\frac{(0.95)(50.0)}{1}=\frac{(M_2)(19.9)}{1}\]

\[M_{2}=\frac{0.95 \times 50.0}{19.9} = 2.38\text{ M}\]

subject: Chemistry
q:
Sulphuric acid, \[\ce{H2SO4}\], is standardised with 25.0 ml of 0.75 M sodium hydroxide, \[\ce{NaOH}\], using a phenolphthalein indicator. The chemical equation for the reaction and the volume of \[\ce{H2SO4}\], used (in ml), are shown below:

\[\ce{H2SO4 + 2 NaOH -> Na2SO4 + 2 H2O}\]

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3
Initial volume (ml) 65.8 50.3 34.0
Final volume (ml) 50.3 34.4 18.5


What is the concentration of \[\ce{H2SO4}\]?
skill: Solve problems based on acid-base titrations, and give equations for neutralisation reactions
feedback:
The correct concentration of \[\ce{H2SO4}\] is 0.60 M.

The first step is to calculate the volume of \[\ce{H2SO4}\] used in each trial to neutralise NaOH. This is done by subtracting the initial volume from the final volume as shown below:

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3
Initial volume (ml) 65.8 50.3 34.0
Final volume (ml) 50.3 34.4 18.5
Used volume (ml) \[65.8 - 50.3 = 15.5\] \[50.3 - 34.4 = 15.9\] \[34.0 - 18.5 = 15.5\]


The second step is to find the average volume of \[\ce{H2SO4}\] by summing the individual volumes and dividing them with the number of trials as shown below:

\[\text{Average = }\frac{15.5+15.9+15.5}{3} = 15.6 \text{ ml}\].

The third step is to use the titration formula to find the concentration of \[\ce{H2SO4}\].

\[\frac{M_{1}V_{1}}{n_{1}}=\frac{M_{2}V_{2}}{n_{2}}\]

Since 1 mole of \[\ce{H2SO4}\] reacts with 2 moles of \[\ce{NaOH}\], as stated in the equation, the value of \[n_1\] is 1, and the value of \[n_2\] is 2.

\[\frac{M_{1}(15.6)}{1}=\frac{(0.75)(25.0)}{2}\]

\[M_{1}=\frac{9.375}{15.6} = 0.60\text{ M}\]

subject: Chemistry
q:
Hydrochloric acid, HCl, is titrated with 15.0 ml of 2.0 M calcium hydroxide, Ca(OH)2, using a phenolphthalein indicator. The chemical equation for the reaction and the volume of HCl used (in ml) are shown below.

\[\ce{Ca(OH)2 + 2 HCl -> CaCl2 + 2 H2O}\]

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3
Initial volume (ml) 50.5 40.1 29.8
Final volume (ml) 40.1 29.8 19.3


What is the concentration of HCl?
skill: Solve problems based on acid-base titrations, and give equations for neutralisation reactions
feedback:
The correct concentration of HCl is 5.77 M.

The first step is to calculate the volume of HCl used in each trial to neutralise Ca(OH)2. This is done by subtracting the initial volume from the final volume as shown below:

Volume Trial 1 Trial 2 Trial 3
Initial volume (ml) 50.5 40.1 29.8
Final volume (ml) 40.1 29.8 19.3
Used volume (ml) \[50.5 - 40.1 = 10.4\] \[40.1 - 29.8 = 10.3\] \[29.8 - 19.3= 10.5\]


The second step is to find the average volume of HCl by summing up the individual volumes and dividing them with the number of trials as shown below:

\[\text{Average HCl volume = }\frac{10.4+10.3+10.5}{3} = 10.4 \text{ ml}\]

The third step is to use the titration formula to find the concentration of HCl. We do not need to convert the volumes from millilitre (ml) to litre (l) because all volumes are in ml.

\[\frac{M_{1}V_{1}}{n_{1}}=\frac{M_{2}V_{2}}{n_{2}}\]

Since 2 moles of HCl react with 1 mole of Ca(OH)2, as stated in the equation, the value of \[n_1\] is 2, and the value of \[n_2\] is 1.

\[\frac{M_{1}(10.4)}{2}=\frac{(2.0)(15.0)}{1}\]

\[M_{1}=\frac{(30.0)(2.0)}{(1)(10.4)} = 5.77\text{ M}\]

subject: Chemistry
q:
Maria performed an acid-base titration to standardise ammonia, NH3 , with 1.50 M hydrochloric acid, HCl, using a phenolphthalein indicator. Identify the correct statements about this experiment.

  1. NH3 should be kept in a conical flask, while HCl should be transferred in a burette.
  2. After neutralisation, the indicator changes from colourless to pink.
  3. The experiment should be performed at least three times.
skill: Solve problems based on acid-base titrations, and give equations for neutralisation reactions
feedback:
The indicator is always put in the titrant (the substance whose concentration is known). Since phenolphthalein is transferred in 1.5 M HCl (which is an acid), it will be initially colourless. After neutralisation, the pH increases and the solution is no longer acidic, so the indicator changes from colourless to pink. The experiment should be performed at least three times to minimise the possibility of human error and to obtain a more accurate concentration value of NH3.

Since NH3 is being standardised and the concentration of HCl is known, HCl should be transferred in a conical flask, while NH3 should be transferred in a burette. This is because a burette can record a highly accurate volume up to 0.1 ml.

subject: Chemistry
q: Bleach is a common base for cleaning toilets. The more basic the bleach is, the better it is at removing stains. How would the performance of bleach change if pure water is added to it?
skill: Describe the amphoteric nature of water using the equation of its self-ionisation
feedback: Recall that water ionises into \[H^+\] ions and \[OH^-\] ions. Therefore, when water is added into bleach, the overall concentration of \[OH^-\] ions in the solution will also decrease reducing the pH. Hence, bleach becomes less basic and its performance becomes poor.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Acid rain occurs due to the mixing of acidic gases with the rainwater. Which of the following statement(s) is true?

  1. Acid rain water has a higher pH than that of distilled water.
  2. Acid rain water has more \[H^+\] ions than \[OH^-\] ions.
  3. Acid rain water has a lower pH than distilled water.
skill: Describe the amphoteric nature of water using the equation of its self-ionisation
feedback: Since acid rain is formed when acidic gases mix with water, it will have more \[H^+\] ions than \[OH^-\] ions. As the \[[H^+]\] ion concentration increases, the pH of acid rain will decrease. Therefore, it will have a lower pH than distilled water.

subject: Chemistry
q: Due to the establishment of a new factory near a lake, fish are dying because of the decreasing pH of water. What should you add to fix this problem?
skill: Describe the amphoteric nature of water using the equation of its self-ionisation
feedback: Since fish are dying due to decreasing pH, we need to increase it. Increasing the pH means reducing the acidity (or increasing the basicity). The higher the pH, the more basic the solution is. The only base in the options given is ammonia \[NH_3\]. The rest are acids.

subject: Chemistry
q: What would happen if the concentration of \[OH^-\] ions increases to more than \[1 \times 10^{-7}\] at \[25^{\circ}C\] in an aqueous solution?
skill: Describe the amphoteric nature of water using the equation of its self-ionisation
feedback: Recall that the concentration of \[OH^-\] ions and \[H^+\] in pure water at \[25^{\circ}C\] is \[1 \times 10^{-7}\]. If it increases even further, it implies that there are more \[OH^-\] ions than \[H^+\] ions in the solution. When \[[OH^-] > [H^+]\], solution is basic. Therefore, solution becomes basic due to an increase in \[[OH^-]\] concentration.

subject: Chemistry
q: What happens if the concentration of \[H^+\] ions increases to more than \[1 \times 10^{-7}\] at \[25^{\circ}C\] in an aqueous solution?
skill: Describe the amphoteric nature of water using the equation of its self-ionisation
feedback: Recall that the concentration of \[H^+\] and \[OH^-\] ions in pure water at \[25^{\circ}C\] is \[1 \times 10^{-7}\]. If \[H^+\] increases even further, it implies that there are more \[H^+\] ions than \[OH^-\] ions in the solution. When \[[H^+] > [OH^-]\], the solution is acidic. Therefore, the solution has turned acidic due to an increase in \[H^+\] ions.

subject: Chemistry
q: Your boss gives you an unknown solution and asks you to find out whether it is basic or acidic. The only information he provides you is that the concentration of \[[H^+]\] is \[1.0 \times 10^{-5} M\].
skill: Classify solutions as neutral, acidic or basic and calculate the pH
feedback:
The formula for the ionization constant of water is \[K_w = [H^+][OH^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}\]. We are given that \[[H^+]\] ions is \[1 \times 10^{-5}M\]. Therefore, we can find \[[OH^-]\] by using the following equation:

\[[OH^-] = \frac{[K_w]}{ [H^+]} =\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}M\]
Since \[[H^+] > [OH^-]\], the solution is acidic.

subject: Chemistry
q: Given that a solution has an \[[OH^-]\] concentration of \[1.0 \times 10^{-4} M\], what can you say about it?
skill: Classify solutions as neutral, acidic or basic and calculate the pH
feedback:
The formula for the ionisation constant of water is \[K_w = [H^+][OH^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}\]. We are given that \[[OH^-]\] ions is \[1 \times 10^{-4}M\]. Therefore, we can find \[[H^+]\] by using the following equation:

\[[H^+] = \frac{[K_w]}{ [OH^-]} =\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-10}M\]

Since \[[OH^-] > [H^+]\], the solution is basic.

subject: Chemistry
q: If the concentration of \[[H^+]\]  ions is \[1 \times 10^{-6}M\] in a solution of ascorbic acid, what is \[[OH^-]\]?
skill: Classify solutions as neutral, acidic or basic and calculate the pH
feedback:
The formula for the ionisation constant of water is \[K_w = [H^+][OH^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}\]. We are given that \[[H^+]\] ions is \[1 \times 10^{-6}M\]. Therefore, we can find \[[OH^-]\] by using the following equation:

\[[OH^-] = \frac{[K_w]}{ [H^+]} =\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8}M\]

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the pH of a solution containing hydroxide ion concentration of \[3 \times 10^{-2}M\]?
skill: Classify solutions as neutral, acidic or basic and calculate the pH
feedback:
First, find the \[H^+\] concentration by using the formula:

\[[H^+] = \frac{[K_w]}{ [OH^-]} =\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{3.0 \times 10^{-2}} = 3.3 \times 10^{-13}M\]

Now calculate the pH by using the following formula:

\[pH = -log([H^+]) = -log(3.3 \times 10^{-13}) = 12.5\]

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the pH of 0.005M HCl solution?
skill: Classify solutions as neutral, acidic or basic and calculate the pH
feedback:
Calculate pH by using the following formula:

\[pH = -log([H^+]) = -log(0.005) = 2.3\]

subject: Chemistry
q:
What can you do to increase the rate of reaction of organic compounds?

I.   increase the temperature
II.  use a catalyst
III. increase the pressure
skill: Define organic compounds, discuss their chemical diversity and magnitude and general characteristics
feedback: The reaction rates of organic compounds are slower than those of inorganic compounds due to the presence of C‐H covalent bonds that are very strong and stable. Breaking those bonds requires a lot of energy and the reaction rates can be increased by providing heat, increasing pressure, and using a catalyst.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following option correctly identifies the covalent bonds in an organic compound shown below as polar or non-polar?
skill: Define organic compounds, discuss their chemical diversity and magnitude and general characteristics
feedback:
When electrons are shared unequally between atoms, a polar is covalent bond formed. This is due to the difference in electronegativity between the two atoms. The more electronegative atom pulls the shared electrons strongly towards itself becoming partially negative. While the less electronegative atom obtains a lower electron density, and becomes partially positive.

When the electronegativity difference between the two atoms is less than 0.5, the covalent bond formed between them is non-polar. Since C and H have almost equal electronegativities, the difference is zero and the bond is non-polar between C— C and C— H. Chlorine (Cl) and Oxygen (O) are more electronegative than C, so C—Cl and C—O bonds are polar.

subject: Chemistry

q: Which statement about organic compounds containing different functional groups is FALSE?
options: Alcohols are non-polar, but alkanes are polar.
Ketones have higher boiling points than alkanes.
skill: Define hydrocarbons and their derivatives, and the IUPAC system
feedback:
Alcohols are POLAR, but alkanes are NON-POLAR. Hydrocarbons (alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes) are non-polar, because they are made up of non-polar covalent bonds. The carbon-carbon and carbon-hydrogen bonds are non-polar, because carbon and hydrogen atoms have very similar electronegativities.

Ketones have higher boiling points than alkanes, because they have stronger intermolecular forces due to dipole-dipole interactions. Amines are basic as they contain a nitrogen atom, which has a lone pair of electrons that can be donated, acting as a base.

q: A hydrocarbon with a molecular formula \[C_4H_{8}\] is:
skill: Define the term hydrocarbons and distinguish between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons (also between alkynes and alkenes based on their unsaturation)
feedback:
Since the molecular formula of the hydrocarbon is \[C_4H_{8}\], we know that it contains 4 carbons. We can use this information to identify whether this hydrocarbon is alkane, alkene, or alkyne as we know their general formulas. Alkanes: \[C_nH_{2n+2}\], Alkenes: \[ C_nH_{2n}\], and Alkynes: \[ C_nH_{2n-2}\] where \[n\] is the number of carbons.

4-carbon alkane: \[C_4H_{2(4)+2} = C_4H_{10}\]

4-carbon alkene: \[C_4H_{2(4)} = C_4H_{8}\]

4-carbon alkyne: \[C_4H_{2(4)-2} = C_4H_{6}\]

Now we know that it was an alkene because only a 4-carbon alkene has a molecular formula of \[C_4H_{8}\].

9-carbon alkane: \[C_9H_{2(9)+2} = C_9H_{20}\]

9-carbon alkene: \[C_9H_{2(9)} = C_9H_{18}\]

9-carbon alkyne: \[C_9H_{2(9)-2} = C_9H_{16}\]

Now we know that it was an alkane because only a 9-carbon alkane has a molecular formula of \[C_9H_{20}\].

q: Which is not a protein\[?\]
topic: Sources and uses of carbs, proteins, and lipids
skill: List the sources and uses of carbohydrates
feedback:
Proteins are large molecules consisting of one or more long chains of amino acids linked together. Enzymes, antibodies, and gelatin are all classified as proteins.
Enzymes are protein molecules that act as catalysts in living organisms, regulating the rate of chemical reactions without being altered themselves.
Antibodies are large, Y-shaped proteins produced by plasma cells that are used by the immune system to neutralise pathogens.
Gelatin is a colourless and flavourless food ingredient. It is made up of protein obtained from animal products.
On the other hand, cholesterol is a fatty substance known as a lipid and is vital for the normal functioning of the body.

skill: Define the term lipids and list the sources and uses of lipids
feedback: Fats and oils are both lipids. Fats are obtained from animals (cows, sheep, goats) to make butter, ghee etc. Oils are obtained from marine animals (salmon and whale) and also from the seeds of plants (olives, sunflower, cotton, corn, coconut, nuts). Starch and sugars are the sources of carbohydrates, not lipids.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following vitamins is NOT an example of lipids?
skill: Define the term lipids and list the sources and uses of lipids
feedback: Only vitamins A, D, E, and K are the examples of lipids. Vitamin C is a water-soluble vitamin which is not an example of lipids. Lipids are insoluble in water.

subject: Chemistry
q: Thousands of amino acids combine to form _____.
skill: Define proteins, describe peptide linkage between amino acids, recognise the functional groups of proteins and explain their denaturing
feedback: Amino acids are organic compounds that combine to form proteins. They are linked by the formation of peptide bonds and form proteins.

subject: Chemistry

skill: Define proteins, describe peptide linkage between amino acids, recognise the functional groups of proteins and explain their denaturing
feedback: Out of the total twenty amino acids that naturally exist, ten of them are classified as nonessential amino acids. Nonessential amino acids can be synthesised or derived within the body from the other essential amino acids.

subject: Chemistry
q: The linkage above, which joins two amino acid units, is called a _____.
skill: Define proteins, describe peptide linkage between amino acids, recognise the functional groups of proteins and explain their denaturing
feedback: The peptide bond is a chemical amide linkage formed by the condensation of the amino group of one amino acid with the carboxyl group of another. Proteins are large molecules consisting of two or more chains of amino acids joined together. These amino acids are linked together through the peptide linkage.

subject: Chemistry
q: Nucleic acids are linear chains of _____.
skill: Define the term nucleic acids, discuss their importance and describe the basic structural features of DNA and RNA
feedback: Nucleic acids are generally very large molecules. Nucleic acids are linear polymers (chains) of nucleotides, which are monomers. Each nucleotide consists of three components: a purine or pyrimidine nucleobase (sometimes termed nitrogenous base), a pentose (five-carbon) sugar, and a phosphate group.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which is not present in DNA\[?\]
skill: Define the term nucleic acids, discuss their importance and describe the basic structural features of DNA and RNA
feedback:
DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, is a long, double-stranded molecule composed of two chains. Each of these chains is made up of a sugar, a phosphate unit, and a nitrogen base. The sugar and phosphate units form the backbone of the chain and the two chains are then linked through the nitrogen bases.
The sugars ribose and deoxyribose are both important components of nucleic acids. Ribose sugar is found in the RNA molecule and deoxyribose sugar is found in the DNA molecule.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which compound found in every living cell serves as the information and control unit?
skill: Define the term nucleic acids, discuss their importance and describe the basic structural features of DNA and RNA
feedback: DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, is a long, double stranded molecule composed of two chains. It is an essential component of every living cell and the permanent storage place and carrier of its genetic information. As a result, it is classified as the compound that is found in every living cell and serves as the information and control unit.

subject: Chemistry
q: Sara is suffering from anaemia, skin diseases, and infertility. What type of diet should be recommended to her to overcome the deficiency of vitamins in her body?
options: wholemeal bread and fresh green vegetables
skill: Describe vitamins, their types, sources, uses and diseases due to their deficiencies
feedback:
Sara should eat more wholemeal bread and fresh green vegetables , because she is suffering from vitamin B and E deficiency.

Vitamin B is required for the development of skin and transmission of nerve impulses. Its deficiency symptoms include anaemia and skin diseases. Lack of vitamin E causes sterility in both men and women. Wholemeal bread, liver, and soybeans are rich sources of vitamin B. Additionally, fresh green vegetables, rice, eggs, and butter are rich in vitamin E.

subject: Chemistry
q: Adeel's red blood cell count is below the normal range, and he also suffers from poor night vision. After conducting numerous tests, a doctor diagnosed that Adeel suffers from dietary deficiencies. What type of diet should be recommended to Adeel to overcome the deficiency of vitamins in his body?
skill: Describe vitamins, their types, sources, uses and diseases due to their deficiencies
feedback:
Adeel should have more oranges and fish oils, because he is suffering from vitamin A and C deficiency.

Vitamin A is required for the development of photopigments in our eyes that help us see clearly during night-time. On the other hand, vitamin C is essential for the formation of red blood cells. Oranges and lemons are rich sources of vitamin C. Fish oils, fresh green vegetables, and milk are rich in vitamin A.

subject: Chemistry
q: Adam has weak bones. While playing sports, he suffered an injury to his arm, which continued to bleed for some time. What type of diet should be recommended to Adam to help him overcome the deficiency of vitamins in his body?
skill: Describe vitamins, their types, sources, uses and diseases due to their deficiencies
feedback:
Adam should have more milk and fresh green vegetables, because he is suffering from vitamin D and K deficiency.

Vitamin D is required for bones and teeth formation, whereas vitamin K is needed for blood clotting. Milk and eggs are a great source of vitamin D, while fresh green vegetables and liver are rich in vitamin K.

subject: Chemistry
q: In which layer of the atmosphere do satellites move in their orbits?
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Thermosphere is the fourth layer of atmosphere which is also the hottest layer. Satellites orbit in this layer but do not get too hot because the pressure in this layer is very low to cause any significant heat damage. When the pressure is very low, heat does not transfer.

subject: Chemistry
q: A meteor from outer space enters Earth. After which layer of the atmosphere does it start burning?
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Mesosphere is the third layer of the atmosphere which is also the coldest layer. When a meteor passes through the mesosphere, it begins to burn because of the friction created between the air molecules in the atmosphere and the surface of meteoroid, creating a lot of heat. There is no air present in the layers above the mesosphere, which are thermosphere and exosphere.

subject: Chemistry
q: An astronaut begins travelling in a rocket which is going to space. While traveling through the layers of the atmosphere, she notices that the temperature drops to the lowest at one point. In which layer of the atmosphere was she present when the temperature dropped to its lowest point?
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Mesosphere is the third layer of the atmosphere which is the coldest layer. The astronaut observed the lowest recorded temperature while traveling through this layer of the atmosphere.

subject: Chemistry
q: Aeroplanes fly in the _____ which also protects us from harmful ultraviolent radiation.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Stratosphere is the second layer of the atmosphere, where airplanes and weather balloons fly. This layer also contains the ozone that stops ultraviolet radiation from reaching us, protecting us from cancer.

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the name of the layer of air where clouds form and changes in weather take place?
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Troposphere is the lowermost and the first layer of the atmosphere where clouds form and changes in weather take place.

subject: Chemistry
q: The uppermost layer of the earth is called the _____.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Exosphere is the fifth and the last layer of the atmosphere. It leads to open space. Stratosphere is the second layer; mesosphere the third; and thermosphere is the fourth layer.

subject: Chemistry
q: Thermosphere is the _____ layer of the atmosphere which is very ____ and ____.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Thermosphere is the fourth layer of the atmosphere. It is very thin and hot.

subject: Chemistry
q: The third layer of the atmosphere is called _____ and it is the _____.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Mesosphere is the third layer of the atmosphere and also the coldest layer. Thermosphere is the fourth layer which is very hot.

subject: Chemistry
q: Ozone is present in the _____ layer of the atmosphere which is called the _____.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: The second layer of the atmosphere is called stratosphere, which is where the ozone layer is found. Ozone protects us from harmful ultraviolent radiations that cause skin cancer and global warming.

subject: Chemistry
q: The lowermost layer of the earth is called _____ and it is the _____ dense part of the atmosphere.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Troposphere is the first and most dense layer of the atmosphere. Dense molecules settle at the bottom because they are the heaviest. Exosphere is the uppermost layer of the earth and it is the least dense, which leads to open space.

subject: Chemistry
q: The blanket of air is called _____ and it has _____ layers.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Atmosphere is called the blanket of air and it has five layers: troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere, and exosphere.

subject: Chemistry
q: The atmosphere is divided into six layers depending upon _____.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: The atmosphere is divided into different layers according to their difference in temperature. The layers varying temperature are called layers of atmosphere. The temperature of the atmosphere changes with height, as we go from ground to space.

subject: Chemistry
q: The second layer of the atmosphere is called the ______.
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: The second layer of atmosphere is called the stratosphere. It lies between 15 km to 50 km from the Earth's surface.

subject: Chemistry
q: Other than gases, what solid particles are present in the air?
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Air is composed of approximately 78 % nitrogen, 21 % oxygen, and 1 % carbon dioxide along with traces of other gases. Some amount of water vapours, smoke, and dust particles are also present in the air.

subject: Chemistry
q: Around 99% of air is made up of
skill: Define atmosphere, state its composition and describe the different layers of atmosphere
feedback: Air is composed of 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen. Together, they form 78 + 21 = 99 % of air.

subject: Chemistry
q: At night, most of the sun's energy
options: helps in the growth of plants.
skill: Define the term greenhouse effect and explain global warming and its effects
feedback: The Earth's atmosphere does the same thing a greenhouse does. Gases in the atmosphere such as carbon dioxide play the same role a roof of a greenhouse does. During the day, the sun shines through the atmosphere and the Earth's surface warms up due to the sunlight. At night, the Earth's surface cools down and releases the heat into space. However, some of the heat is trapped by the greenhouse gases in the atmosphere. This is what keeps our Earth warm.

subject: Chemistry

skill: Define the term greenhouse effect and explain global warming and its effects
feedback: A greenhouse is a house made of glass. It has glass walls and a glass roof. Sunlight enters and refracts, thus getting trapped inside. Along with sunlight, heat also enters the greenhouse, thus making it warm inside. People grow tomatoes, flowers, and other plants inside a greenhouse under controlled climatic conditions. A greenhouse stays warm even during winter.

subject: Chemistry

skill: Define the term greenhouse effect and explain global warming and its effects

For example, factors such as projected increases in temperatures, changes in rainfall patterns, extreme weather conditions, and a decrease in the availability of water may all result in reduced agricultural productivity.

increase in the average temperature of the Earth
a decrease in the amount of carbon dioxide

subject: Chemistry

skill: Explain ozone formation and list its uses, and describe ozone depletion and its effects

q:
How does acid rain destroys buildings?
  1. It causes the corrosion of metals.
  2. It dissolve stones e.g. marble and limestone.
  3. It causes earthquakes.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is a cause of acid rain?
options: throwing garbage into the ocean and sea
burning fossil fuels on a large scale
topic: Formation and effects of acid rain
skill: Explain how acid rain forms and describe its effects on the environment
feedback:
Acid rain is caused by pollutants that are released into the air from fossil fuels burning on a large scale, which releases sulphur dioxide, carbon dioxide, and nitrogen oxides into the atmosphere.

Throwing garbage in the ocean results in water pollution. Using chlorofluorocarbons as refrigerants or in aerosols depletes the ozone layer, which causes global warming. Generating electricity using wind energy does not involve burning fossil fuels, so it does not cause acid rain.

subject: Chemistry
q:
How does acid rain destroy marine (aquatic) life?
  1. It increases the acidity of water in rivers and lakes.
  2. It increases the temperature of water in rivers and lakes.
  3. It destroys the ozone layer causing global warming.
  4. It introduces toxic metals in rivers and lakes.
topic: Formation and effects of acid rain
skill: Explain how acid rain forms and describe its effects on the environment
feedback: I and IV only. Acid rain increases the acidity of water, which is harmful for fish and other marine life. Acid rain also extracts toxic metals e.g. aluminium from the soil and releases it in lakes and rivers, which is also harmful for marine life.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following gases does NOT cause acid rain?
topic: Formation and effects of acid rain
skill: Explain how acid rain forms and describe its effects on the environment
feedback:
Chlorofluorocarbons do NOT cause acid rain. They damage the ozone layer, causing global warming.

When \[\ce{CO2}\] reacts with the rainwater, it forms carbonic acid. The reaction of \[\ce{SO2}\] and \[\ce{NO2}\] with the rainwater causes the formation sulphuric acid and nitric acid, respectively. Therefore, these three gases cause acid rain which destroys forests, marine life, and buildings.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following can occur naturally?

I.   greenhouse effect
II.  acid rain
III. deforestation
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects
feedback:
The greenhouse effect, acid rain, and deforestation all may occur naturally.

The greenhouse effect occurs naturally due to the layer of gases present in our atmosphere. This layer of gases allows heat to enter the Earth and stops it from moving back out. Thus, the Earth's temperature is maintained at night in the absence of the Sun.
The natural causes of acid rain are volcanoes and decaying vegetation. Volcanoes release sulphur dioxide gas.
Deforestation can occur naturally due to drought or wildfire.

q: Abdullah collects a sample of acid rain and put litmus paper into it. What will happen to the litmus paper?

The blue litmus paper remained blue.
The red litmus paper turned green.
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects
feedback:
Acid rain is any form of precipitation that is acidic.
Acids turn blue litmus paper to red.
We can test for the acidic nature of acid rain by putting blue litmus paper in it.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following measures can be taken to stop acid rain?

I.   riding bicycles or walking instead of using automobiles
II.  using wind turbines and solar panels to generate electricity
III. by planting more trees
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects
feedback:
Acid rain is mainly caused by the sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxides that is released when fuel is burned.
Thermal power stations and industries contribute the most towards causing acid rain.
We can reduce the amount of acid rain by generating electricity using wind and solar power stations and by using bicycles or walking where possible.

Planting more trees is not helpful in reducing acid rain, because plants can't absorb sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxide. Rather, the growth of plants is also affected by acid rain.

subject: Chemistry
q: Solid waste should be managed properly by landfilling, _____, or recycling.
skill: Recommend strategies that the governments adopt to control air pollution
feedback: Solid waste pollutes the Earth's natural environment when it is dumped into open spaces or thrown in rivers or oceans. Hence, we should not dump waste in open places. Solid waste should be managed properly by landfill, incineration, or recycling.

subject: Chemistry
q: When solid waste is _____, it emits harmful gases.
skill: Recommend strategies that the governments adopt to control air pollution
feedback: When solid waste is burned, it emits harmful gases into the air.

subject: Chemistry
q: Recycling can help reduce _____.
options: the amount of oxygen in the atmosphere
skill: Recommend strategies that the governments adopt to control air pollution
feedback: Through recycling, we can make new things from used items and can also reduce waste disposal and pollution. Therefore, it is an alternative to waste disposal, which saves material and helps lower greenhouse gas emissions.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is NOT a property of pure water?
options: It does not have a smell.
It does not have a taste.
skill: Describe the composition and properties of water and explain what makes it a universal solvent
feedback: All the options are correct. Pure water is odourless, which means it does not have a smell. Moreover, it is tasteless and transparent, therefore, it looks clear.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following will NOT dissolve in water?
skill: Describe the composition and properties of water and explain what makes it a universal solvent
feedback: Hexane (\[\ce{C6H14}\]) will NOT dissolve in water because it is a non-polar solvent. Hexane contains non-polar \[\ce{-C-C -}\] and \[\ce{-C-H -}\] covalent bonds. Since polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents only, hexane does not dissolve in water.

subject: Chemistry
q: At most, how many hydrogen bonds can one molecule of water form?
skill: Describe the composition and properties of water and explain what makes it a universal solvent
feedback: Theoretically speaking, one molecule of water can at most form 4 hydrogen bonds. This is because oxygen contains two lone pairs, both of which can develop a force of attraction with the partially positively charged hydrogen atoms in the neighbouring water molecules. Similarly, since a water molecule (\[\ce{H2O}\]) also contains two hydrogen (H) atoms, the hydrogen atoms can also form hydrogen bonds with the partially negatively charged oxygen atom in the neighbouring water molecules.

subject: Chemistry
q: Why does ice float on water?
options: Molecules in ice occupy more space, thus, increasing its density.
Molecules in ice occupy less space, thus, decreasing its density.
Molecules in ice occupy less space, thus, increasing its density.
Molecules in ice occupy more space, thus, decreasing its density.
skill: Describe the composition and properties of water and explain what makes it a universal solvent
feedback: Ice floats on water because molecules in ice occupy more space, thus, decreasing its density. In ice, water molecules are arranged in hexagonal geometric patterns that take up more space. Therefore, the volume increases and density decreases. Ice floats on water as its density is lower than the density of water.

subject: Chemistry
q: Why do our bodies require only a small amount of water to remove extra body heat and maintain a healthy body temperature?
options: because water is a polar molecule
because water has a high heat capacity
because water has a high heat of vaporisation
because water is a natural solvent
skill: Describe the composition and properties of water and explain what makes it a universal solvent
feedback: Our bodies require only a small amount of water to remove extra body heat because water has a high heat of vaporisation. Heat of vaporisation is the amount of heat required to change the state of a liquid to gas. Water has a high heat of vaporisation, therefore, when sweat evaporates, only a small amount of water is lost, and a large amount of body heat is removed. Therefore, water is important for regulating the body temperature.

subject: Chemistry
q:

I. surface water
II. underground water
III. rain water
IV. seawater
skill: Describe the sources and explain the importance of water in the environment
feedback: Underground water needs to be dug out or accessed through pipes and wells. Whereas surface water is easy to access from lakes, rivers, rain water, and the oceans.

subject: Chemistry
q: Oceans contain more water than _____.
skill: Describe the sources and explain the importance of water in the environment
feedback: Oceans are the biggest water reservoirs. They have more water than all other water reservoirs on the surface of, and under, the ground.

subject: Chemistry
q: Lakes and ponds are examples of _____.

feedback: Lakes and ponds are found on the surface of the earth. Therefore, they are examples of surface water.

subject: Chemistry
q: The total amount of water present on earth is about _____ billion cubic kilometres.

feedback: About \[71\] percent of the Earth's surface is covered with water. In terms of volume, all of the water present on Earth is about \[1.333\] billion cubic kilometers (km\[^{3}\]).

subject: Chemistry
q: The fresh water available to animals is only _____ of the total water on Earth.
skill: Describe the sources and explain the importance of water in the environment

subject: Chemistry

Scum is a layer of dirt or froth on the surface of a liquid. In contrast, soft water does not form scum because it does not contain these dissolved minerals.

q: The correct formula of magnesium hydrogen carbonate is
skill: Differentiate between soft and hard water and describe the disadvantages of hard water
feedback: The correct formula of magnesium hydrogen carbonate is \[\ce{Mg(HCO_3)_2}\]. It is composed of one magnesium (Mg2+) ion and two bicarbonate (HCO3-) ions.

subject: Chemistry
q: Alice washed her hands with soap. She noticed that water formed lather quite easily. What type of water does she use to wash her hands?
options: It can either be hard or soft water.
There is not enough information provided to answer this question.
skill: Differentiate between soft and hard water and describe the disadvantages of hard water
feedback: Alice must be using soft water to wash her hands. Water that forms lather easily with soap is called soft water. It is difficult to make lather with hard water because it contains dissolved minerals that form salts, thus, preventing the formation of lather.

subject: Chemistry
q: How is zeolite recycled after binding to calcium and magnesium ions, (\[\ce{Ca^{+2}}\] and \[\ce{Mg^{+2}}\]), that cause permanent hardness in water?
options: by heating with concentrated sodium chloride
There is not enough information provided to answer.
skill: Describe methods for eliminating temporary and permanent hardness of water
feedback:
Zeolite is recycled after binding to calcium and magnesium ions by heating it with concentrated sodium chloride. When hard water is passed through the ion-exchange resin, \[\ce{Ca^{+2}}\] and \[\ce{Mg^{+2}}\] ions bind with the zeolite (\[\ce{Na2Z}\]) beads, whereas water passes through the resin.

\[\ce{Ca^{+2} + Na2Z -> 2 Na+ + CaZ}\]

\[\ce{Mg^{+2} + Na2Z -> 2 Na+ + MgZ}\]

This used zeolite is recycled after being heated with a solution of concentrated sodium chloride as shown below.

\[\ce{CaZ + 2 NaCl -> Na2Z + CaCl2}\]

\[\ce{MgZ + 2 NaCl -> Na2Z + MgCl2}\]

subject: Chemistry
q: The chemical name of zeolite is _____ and it is used to remove _____ from water.
skill: Describe methods for eliminating temporary and permanent hardness of water
feedback: The chemical name of zeolite is sodium aluminium silicate and it is used to remove permanent hardness from water. Zeolite is used in ion exchange resin to remove calcium and magnesium ions, (\[\ce{Ca^{+2}}\] and \[\ce{Mg^{+2}}\]), that cause permanent hardness in water. It is commonly denoted by \[\ce{Na2Z}\].

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the chemical formula of washing soda that is used to remove temporary hardness in water?
skill: Describe methods for eliminating temporary and permanent hardness of water
feedback: \[\ce{Na2CO3}\] is commercially known as washing soda. Its chemical name is sodium carbonate. It can be used to remove both temporary and permanent hardness from water.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following salts will cause temporary hardness in water?
  1. \[\ce{MgSO4}\] and \[\ce{CaSO4}\]
  2. \[\ce{MgCl2}\] and \[\ce{CaCl2}\]
  3. \[\ce{Mg(HCO3)2}\] and \[\ce{Ca(HCO3)2}\]
skill: Describe methods for eliminating temporary and permanent hardness of water
feedback: The correct answer is III only. Temporary hardness in water is caused by the presence of calcium and/or magnesium hydrogen carbonates, \[\ce{Ca(HCO3)2}\] and \[\ce{Mg(HCO3)2}\]. Permanent hardness in water is caused by the presence of calcium/magnesium sulphates or chlorides.

subject: Chemistry
q: Why is it not possible to remove permanent hardness in water by boiling?
options: Sulphate salts are insoluble in water.
Bicarbonate salts are insoluble in water.
Sulphate salts do not decompose easily upon heating.
Bicarbonates do not decompose upon heating.
skill: Describe methods for eliminating temporary and permanent hardness of water
feedback: Boiling does not remove permanent hardness, because sulphate salts do not decompose easily upon heating. Permanent hardness in water is caused by the presence of calcium/magnesium sulphates or chlorides. Temporary hardness, however, is caused by the presence of calcium and/or magnesium hydrogen carbonates that easily decompose upon heating.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The government's recent ban on the use of plastic bags can help reduce _____.

I. water pollution
II. land pollution
III. air pollution
skill: Recognise the water pollutant wastes, and describe their effects on life
feedback: Plastics are non-biodegradable. They are usually buried in landfills where they release toxic chemicals into the soil and groundwater for many years. They are also burned in incinerators, releasing harmful gases into the air. A lot of plastic waste also ends up in the seas and oceans, causing water pollution.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following phrases BEST describes the image given below?
options: the death of aquatic animals due to water pollution
a variety of fish swimming in a clean pond
sea plants growing in a marshy area
skill: Recognise the water pollutant wastes, and describe their effects on life
feedback: The above image shows the death of aquatic animals due to water pollution. When water is polluted by contaminants such as household and industrial waste, it becomes hazardous for human, animal, and plant life. This results in the death of aquatic animals such as fish through suffocation, poisoning, and diseases.

subject: Chemistry
q: Household waste, when released into water bodies, poses a threat to _____.
skill: Recognise the water pollutant wastes, and describe their effects on life
feedback: Household waste contains pollutants such as detergents, soaps, and sewage. Detergents are non-biodegradable and contain phosphates that lead to rapid algal growth. When they die, decaying algae consume oxygen in large quantities, thus depleting oxygen for other aquatic life. Sewage contains impurities and disease-causing microbes that adversely impact the quality of water, thus, making it unfit for aquatic life.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following can lead to water pollution?

I. household waste
II. chlorination of water
III. natural gas production
IV. biodiversity in oceans
skill: Recognise the water pollutant wastes, and describe their effects on life
feedback:
The correct response is I & III.

Harmful human activities result in the contamination of surface and groundwater. Water pollution is therefore e caused by household waste, agriculture waste, livestock waste, pesticides, oil leaks, detergents, and natural gas production.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which human activity does NOT result in the contamination of water bodies?
skill: Recognise the water pollutant wastes, and describe their effects on life
feedback:
Agriculture, which includes livestock, is one of the leading causes of water pollution. Water runoff from farms carries away manure, fertilizers, ammonia, pesticides and livestock waste, all of which contaminate water bodies.

In septic systems, wastewater drains from toilets and sinks ultimately into nearby water bodies. If not handled properly, untreated waste can end up polluting them.

The recycling of plastic does not result in the contamination of water bodies.

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the purpose of aeration during the treatment of sewage water?
options: to provide carbon dioxide to aerobic bacteria
to provide carbon dioxide to anaerobic bacteria
to provide oxygen to aerobic bacteria
skill: Explain the methods of water treatment and describe the causes, symptoms, and preventive measures of various water borne diseases
feedback: While treating sewage water, aeration is carried out to provide oxygen to aerobic bacteria. Aeration occurs during the activated sludge process, which is the third step of treatment. After sewage water is passed through sand and gravel filters, it is blown with air. This results in the formation of large, porous clumps that absorb different contaminants. Air is important for aerobic bacteria, which need oxygen to respire. Aerobic bacteria break down organic matter present in sewage water, such as fat, oil, carbohydrates, proteins, and urea, into inorganic matter.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following processes happens during the activated sludge treatment?
skill: Explain the methods of water treatment and describe the causes, symptoms, and preventive measures of various water borne diseases
feedback: Aeration occurs during the activated sludge treatment, which is the third step of wastewater treatment. After sewage water is passed through sand and gravel filters, it is blown with air. This results in the formation of large, porous clumps that absorb different contaminants. Air is also important for aerobic bacteria because it needs oxygen to respire. Aerobic bacteria break down organic matter present in sewage water, such as fat, oil, carbohydrates, proteins, and urea, into inorganic matter.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following processes happens during the secondary sewage treatment?
skill: Explain the methods of water treatment and describe the causes, symptoms, and preventive measures of various water borne diseases
feedback: Filtration occurs during the secondary sewage treatment, which is the second step of wastewater treatment. Water is passed through filters of sand and gravel to remove fine particles, taste, smell, and most of the pathogens.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which ONE of the following processes happens during the primary sewage treatment?
skill: Explain the methods of water treatment and describe the causes, symptoms, and preventive measures of various water borne diseases
feedback: Sedimentation occurs during the primary sewage treatment, which is the first step of wastewater treatment. Water is allowed to stand in big tanks for solids, also known as sludge, so that large suspended particles can settle at the bottom of the tank.

subject: Chemistry
q: During the treatment of raw water, _____ is the process of killing bacteria.
skill: Explain the methods of water treatment and describe the causes, symptoms, and preventive measures of various water borne diseases
feedback:
During, the process of chlorination, water is treated with chlorine to kill bacteria. During sedimentation, water is first allowed to stand so that large, suspended particles can settle to the bottom of a body of water. During coagulation, water is treated with slaked lime and alum to clump together small dirt particles and bacteria. During filtration, water is passed through sand and gravel to remove clumped particles.
The correct order of processes is: sedimentation → coagulation → filtration → chlorination.

subject: Chemistry
q: The apparatus shown below is used for the _____ of ores.
skill: Describe metallurgical operations
feedback:
The apparatus shown below is used for the floatation of ores.

Flotation involves treating the ore and the gangue with water and an oil-detergent mixture. Once the ore is pulverised, it is transferred into a large tank containing water and an oil-detergent mixture. This mixture is treated with compressed air. The particles of ore bind with the detergent and rise to the surface of the water in the form of froth. However, the gangue particles settle at the bottom of the water. Froth containing ore particles is then skimmed from the top of the water-oil mixture.

subject: Chemistry
q: The apparatus shown below is used for the _____ of ores.
skill: Describe metallurgical operations
feedback:
The apparatus shown below is used for the cyclone separation of ores.

A cyclone separator works by separating the ore from the gangue due to a significant difference between their densities. Air is blown at high pressures into a large funnel containing impure ore in pulverised form. As the impurities in the gangue have lower densities, they are blown away by the air, while, the mineral particles remain in the funnel.

subject: Chemistry
q: Given that an ore has a density of \[\ce{10 g / cm^3}\], and the impurities have a density of \[\ce{5 g / cm^3}\], what is the most suitable method to concentrate the ore?
skill: Describe metallurgical operations
feedback: The ore can be concentrated in a cyclone separator. A cyclone separator works by separating the ore from the gangue due to a significant difference between their densities. Air is blown at high pressures into a large funnel containing impure ore in pulverised form. As the impurities in the gangue have lower densities, they are blown away by the air, while, the mineral particles remain in the funnel.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following methods would be the most suitable to concentrate magnetite (\[\ce{Fe3O4}\]) ore?
skill: Describe metallurgical operations
feedback: Since magnetite has magnetic properties, it can be concentrated in a magnetic separator. A magnetic separator works by attracting metal ore with magnets over a belt, whereas, impurities such as gangue are left behind.

skill: Describe metallurgical operations

Once the ore is pulverised, it is transferred into a large tank containing water and an oil-detergent mixture. This mixture is treated with compressed air. The particles of ore bind with the detergent and rise to the surface of the water in the form of froth. However, the gangue particles settle at the bottom of the water. Froth containing ore particles is then skimmed from the top of the water-oil mixture.

skill: Describe metallurgical operations

Air is blown at high pressures into a large funnel containing impure ore in pulverised form. As the impurities in the gangue have lower densities, they are blown away by the air, while, the mineral particles remain in the funnel.
